back_translation: After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.
Job said:
'May the day on which I was born perish, and the night that said, "A boy is conceived."
May that day be darkness; may God above not regard it, and may no light shine upon it.
May darkness and the shadow of death claim it; may a cloud settle over it, and may the blackness of the day terrify it.
As for that night, may thick darkness seize it; may it not be counted among the days of the year nor come into the number of the months.
Behold, may that night be barren; may no joyful voice come into it.
May those skilled at cursing days curse that night—those ready to rouse Leviathan.
May the morning stars of that night grow dark; may it wait for light and find none, and may it not see the rays of dawn.
Because that night did not shut the doors of my mother's womb, nor hide trouble from my eyes.
Why did I not die in the womb, and why did I not breathe my last as I came out of the belly?
Why were there knees to receive me, and breasts for me to nurse?
For if so, by now I would be lying down in peace; I would be asleep and at rest,
together with kings and counselors of the earth, who built structures now in ruins,
or with princes who had gold, who filled their houses with silver.
Or why was I not like a stillborn child that was buried, like infants who never saw the light?
There the wicked cease from making trouble, and there the weary find rest.
The captives are at ease together; they no longer hear the voice of the oppressor.
Both small and great are there, and the slave is free from his master.
Why is light given to one who suffers, and life to the bitter of soul,
who wait for death but it does not come, and dig for it more than for hidden treasure,
who rejoice exceedingly and are glad when they find the grave?
Why is life given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has hedged in on every side?
For my sighing comes instead of food, and my groans pour out like water.
For what I feared has come upon me, and what I dreaded has befallen me.
I am not at peace, nor quiet, nor at rest; only trouble comes.'
